sections.title: Journey of the Grains
sections.content_summary: This section traces the journey of a grain — rice or wheat — from sowing to the dining plate, depicted through a 12-step picture sequence. It shows the contributions of soil, water, sunlight, earthworms, farmers, cattle, mill workers, shopkeepers, and family cooks in making food available. Students are introduced to the idea that multiple elements — natural and human — work together to complete this journey. Indian cultural practices of showing gratitude to 'Mother Nature' through festivals such as Makar Sankranti, Akshay Tritya, Raja Utsav, Onam, and Lohri are highlighted in a 'Do you know?' box.

tasks.task_text: Connect the food items shown below with the ingredients used to make them.
Observe the pictures and answer the following questions. 1. Put a tick (✓) on the elements that assist a grain in its journey. Soil Stone Air Wood Water Tiger / Farmer Earthworm Hills Fire Insect Bat
What is the role of farmers in this process?
List out the missing elements in the table above.
Thank You Everyone! Whom would you like to thank for the food you eat? Please write your answers in the following space.

sections.title: Mindful Eating
sections.content_summary: This section introduces the concept of mindful eating through a classroom narrative where a teacher guides students to experience eating a peanut with eyes closed, using all senses — touch, taste, smell, and texture. Students learn to slow down, pay attention to each bite, and appreciate food. Mindful eating is described as a practice that helps make healthier choices and enjoy food better.

tasks.task_text: What role does your tongue play in this process?
What do your teeth do to the food?
What happens to the food inside your mouth during this process?

sections.title: Valuing Food
sections.content_summary: This section emphasises that every grain is precious and wasting food disrespects the hard work of everyone who contributed to bringing it to the plate. Students are asked to track their food consumption over a week — recording what they eat for breakfast, lunch, and dinner, and noting whether they ate fully or wasted food. The section prompts reflection on how to respond when offered excess food or food one dislikes.

tasks.task_text: For a week, keep a record of the food you eat for breakfast, lunch and dinner. After every meal, make an entry in the following table. If you ate everything, write 'Yes' and add a happy smiley. If you left some food, write 'No' and add a sad face.
If someone offers you food more than you need?
If you do not like a food item on your plate?

sections.title: Enjoy Sports
sections.content_summary: This section highlights the importance of regular physical activity — playing outdoors, running, jumping, cycling, skipping — for staying strong and energetic. Students observe a picture of a park scene showing various sports and playground activities. Traditional Indian games (depicted through pictures) and yoga poses are introduced, with yoga described as improving posture and keeping one happy and active. Reference is made to 'Khel Yoga', the Physical Education and Well-being textbook.

tasks.task_text: Look at the picture below. Identify the activities people are engaged in.
Which physical activities do you do in your daily life?
How do you feel on a day when you play a lot?
Can you name the games depicted in the pictures below?
With your teacher's guidance, practice the easy yoga poses given below for a week, and record your observations.

sections.title: Sleep Well too!
sections.content_summary: This section explains that adequate sleep is as essential as healthy eating and exercise for overall well-being. Students record their sleep timings over three days and note how they felt the next day. Insufficient sleep is described as causing tiredness, difficulty focusing, and irritability. The section reinforces the three pillars of healthy living: eating right, exercising, and sleeping well.

tasks.task_text: Fill the table given below. (Day / When did you sleep? / When did you get up? / Duration of sleep / How you felt during the day? a: Felt fresh; b: Lazy; c: Sleepy)
Why do you think our body needs rest?
Do you think it is important to rest after a busy day of exercise and play? Why?

sections.title: Feeling Safe
sections.content_summary: This section introduces children to the concept of safe and unsafe touch. Students view pictures of different situations and identify whether a child would feel comfortable or uncomfortable. The distinction between a safe touch (e.g. a comforting hug from a family member) and an unsafe touch (one that makes a child feel uneasy, scared, or confused — whether from someone known or a stranger) is clearly drawn. Children are empowered to say 'No', speak up to a trusted adult (teacher or parent), and understand they should not feel uncomfortable or guilty.

tasks.task_text: Look at the pictures of different situations given below. Put a tick (✓) for the situation where the child would feel comfortable and put a cross (✗) for the situation where the child would feel uncomfortable.
What would you do if someone's touch made you feel uncomfortable?
If you face an unsafe situation, who would you tell?
List two situations where you would firmly say 'No'.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: This closing reflection section asks students to synthesise the chapter's learnings across food, body care, and safety. Students consider the impact of missing elements (soil, water, sunlight, cattle, earthworms, farmer, shopkeeper, cook) on a food grain's journey, design their own balanced meal plates for breakfast, lunch, and dinner, and reflect on mindful eating experiences. They also plan habits for daily well-being — including waking early, yoga, balanced diet, exercise, limiting screen time, reading, and sleeping peacefully.

tasks.task_text: If the following are missing, what would happen to a food grain? (Elements: Soil, Water, Sunlight / Animals: Cattle, Earthworm / People: Farmer, Shopkeeper, Cook)
Design your own favourite plate of balanced diet with the food items you like. (Remember we should not waste food.) Breakfast plate / Lunch plate / Dinner plate
Which activities did you enjoy during your 'mindful eating'?
How can you keep your body strong? Write down the names of the exercises and games you would practice.

Which habits would you practice daily? Indicate your plans. (Activity / Your plan — Wake up early, Yoga and exercises, Eat a balanced diet on time, Exercise and play, Less use of TV and mobile, Read a book, Sleep peacefully)

dual_strand.natural: Physical and biological needs of the body — nutrition, physical activity, and sleep as foundations of health
dual_strand.human_cultural: Cultural gratitude for food (harvest festivals), mindful food practices, valuing contributors to the food chain, and social safety awareness (safe vs unsafe touch)
